data.title: European Markets Set for Higher Open
data.description:
European equity markets were on track for a higher open Thursday, buoyed by strong overnight gains in US and Asian markets, where technology stocks led the rebound.

Momentum was driven by reports that the US has lifted export restrictions on chip design software to China, as part of a broader trade agreement emphasizing renewed cooperation on rare earths and advanced technologies.

Investors are also turning their attention to the upcoming US jobs report, which could shape expectations for the Federal Reserve’s next rate move.

A softer-than-expected reading may reinforce bets on a rate cut as early as July.

In Europe, the focus will be on fresh business activity data from Spain and Italy.

In premarket trade, Euro Stoxx 50 and Stoxx 600 futures were both up around 0.2%.

data.title: XS.com Opens New Kuwait Office to Provide Local Support and Market Development
data.description:
XS.com has expanded further into the Middle East by opening a new office in Kuwait—its first in the country. The broker will use the new location as a central base for customer support and market development.

Expansion through Local Partnership

Announced today (Thursday), the contracts for differences (CFDs) broker entered the Gulf country via a partnership with NVEST, a subsidiary of Construction Group and Al Wataniya Group.

A new entity, XS Online, has been formed in Kuwait.Mohamad Ibrahim, Group CEO at XS.com

“This partnership goes beyond an office opening; it’s a strategic step to strengthen our connection with the Arab region,” said Mohamad Ibrahim, Group CEO of XS.com.

“The Kuwaiti market is active, growing, and modern. Our presence here allows us to be closer to our clients, respond faster to market needs, and build our brand across the GCC.”

NVEST’s CEO, Jamal Al-Sabah, said the partnership would support XS’s operations in Kuwait. The broker also said the Kuwait office would help shape regional plans, build local partnerships, and boost its marketing and communication efforts.

Read more: XS.com Gains South Africa License by Acquiring a Local Firm

A Growing Market for Retail Trading

Kuwait has a population of 4.8 million but shows strong demand in retail trading. According to Boursa Kuwait, retail investors made up 38 per cent of the total traded value in 2024. The number of active trading accounts rose 19 per cent in the first half of 2024 to around 20,000.

Although many CFD brokers—including Exness, AvaTrade, XM, and IC Markets—onboard clients from Kuwait, the country’s financial regulator does not provide a specific licence for CFDs. Brokers typically operate on a cross-border basis, following the broader rules for securities.

There are also local Kuwaiti brokers, like NCM Invest, that offers CFDs and other instruments to retail traders. NCM gained a UK license last year.

Meanwhile, other CFD brokers are also expanding across the Gulf. Most are setting up offices and applying for licences in Dubai, while some are also moving into nearby countries.

data.title: German Bunds Could Struggle Amid Spanish, French Bond Supply — Market Talk
data.description: German Bunds could struggle in the morning with significant volumes of government bond issuance coming from Spain and France, says Commerzbank Research's Erik Liem in a note. Eurozone bonds were sold Wednesday on the back of headwinds out of the U.K., although Bunds remain a relative outperformer versus its eurozone peers, according to Tradeweb data. The gilt selloff pushed the 10-year Bund yield 5 bps higher to 2.658% by market close and also led to a wider 10-year gilt-Bund yield spread. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: TradFi body urges SEC reject special treatment for tokenized stocks
data.description:
An industry trade group is urging the US securities regulator to reject a wave of relief requests from crypto companies seeking to offer tokenized stocks. 

The Securities Industry and Financial Markets Association (SIFMA), which consists of securities issuers and finance firms, said in a letter on Monday that it has a “significant concern” about reports of crypto firms submitting no-action or exemptive relief to allow them to offer tokenized equities or securities.

No-action relief would mean the SEC wouldn’t recommend taking enforcement action against a firm over products it launches. Exemptive relief allows the SEC to exclude some products from securities laws to test them.

In the letter to the Securities and Exchange Commission’s Crypto Task Force, SIFMA claimed that if such reliefs were granted, then crypto firms could offer securities to the public “outside of the regulatory structure established by the federal securities laws and from which many critical investor protections flow.”

“The SEC should reject such requests to make significant changes to the regulatory structure for the securities markets under the federal securities laws through immediate no-action or exemptive relief in lieu of a more substantive notice and comment process,” SIFMA said.

“These policy questions are simply too important to be addressed purely through immediate no-action or exemptive requests, and such requests should be rejected.”

SEC considering tokenized securities rule change

SIFMA’s letter comes after SEC Commissioner and Crypto Task Force leader Hester Peirce said in May that the regulator is “considering a potential exemptive order” for firms using blockchain to “issue, trade, and settle securities.”

She said companies looking to create platforms for tokenized securities may have to register with the SEC, which many could consider too expensive and could mean companies don’t issue tokenized securities due to the limited platforms they could trade on.

“Exemptive relief could help resolve this chicken-and-egg problem,” Peirce said.

She added that firms should “not have to comply with inapt regulations, which, in many cases, were developed well before the technologies being tested existed.”

TradFi won’t “share power lightly”

Alexander Grieve, the vice president of government affairs at venture firm Paradigm, wrote to X on Wednesday that SIFMA members “want to protect their market position,” as tokenized securities could see many more platforms offer trading on what are essentially stocks.

He added that for every regulation topic and technological advancement, “there’s incumbent opposition,” such as banks broadly opposing stablecoins and crypto derivatives having traditional finance counterparts in markets like that from CME Group.

“The old gods of finance do not share power lightly.”

Bill Hughes, a lawyer and the global regulatory lead at blockchain software firm Consensys, said on X that “SIFMA’s primary argument is procedural and a reasonable one at that.”

“If we are going to be changing substantive rules on how retail participants can access securities — specifically publicly traded stock, then we should be doing that through notice and comment rulemaking and not particularized exemptive relief or no-action assurances.”

“It seems pretty clear, having certain assets with one foot in the less intermediated and controlled crypto world and the other in the heavily intermediated and controlled tradfi capital market is a regulatory policy mess,” Hughes said. 

“Conundrums abound. We got a lot to figure out,” he added.

Coinbase and Kraken eye tokenized stocks

Crypto exchanges Coinbase and Kraken have looked to launch tokenized securities trading in the US with SEC approval.

Coinbase’s chief legal officer, Paul Grewal, reportedly said the exchange was seeking approval for “tokenized equities,” and that this was a “huge priority” for Coinbase.

On Monday, Kraken began offering tokenized stock trading on its platform, serving up tokens fully backed by shares in major US stocks such as Apple and Microsoft.

However, Kraken didn’t make the service available for users in the US, Canada, the EU, the UK or Australia.

data.title: UNO Minda Completes Acquisition Of IP Rights, E-Drive Business Assets In Germany From Friwo Group Entities
data.description:
UNO Minda Ltd :

  • COMPLETES ACQUISITION OF IP RIGHTS, E-DRIVE BUSINESS ASSETS FROM FRIWO GROUP ENTITIES

  • ACQUISITION OF IP RIGHTS AND E-DRIVE BUSINESS ASSETS IN GERMANY

  • ACQUISITION OF E-DRIVES ASSETS AT VIETNAM FROM FRIWO TO BE COMPLETED BY JULY 15

data.title: Beforepay Group's Carrington Labs Secures US Deal With Doc2Doc Lending
data.description:
Beforepay Group said that its enterprise software division, Carrington Labs, has been selected by US lender Doc2Doc Lending to deliver credit risk-modeling services, marking a key milestone in its international expansion strategy, according to a Thursday filing with the Australian bourse.

Carrington Labs will provide cash flow underwriting to support Doc2Doc's US lending via a usage-based SaaS license, with the financial impact currently unpredictable due to variable factors, the filing said.

data.title: Riyad Bank to Issue Tier 2 Trust Certificates
data.description:
Riyad Bank (SASE:1010) plans to issue US dollar-denominated Tier 2 trust certificates via a special purpose vehicle, targeting eligible investors in Saudi Arabia and abroad.

The Saudi Arabian lender said Thursday the offer under its international trust certificate issuance program is intended to support general banking purposes and requires regulatory approval. The offer's precise amount and terms will be contingent upon prevailing market conditions.

Riyad Bank appointed Standard Chartered Bank, HSBC Bank, Merrill Lynch International, J.P. Morgan Securities, SMBC Group, Mizuho International, DBS Bank, and Riyad Capital as joint lead managers for the proposed issue.

data.title: New York Times business news - July 3
data.description:
The following are the top stories on the New York Times business pages. Reuters has not verified these stories and does not vouch for their accuracy.

- The United States will place a lower-than-promised 20% tariff on many Vietnamese exports, cooling tensions with its tenth-biggest trading partner days before the U.S. president could raise levies on most imports.

- Del Monte Foods kicked off efforts to pursue a sale as it filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy proceedings in the U.S. under an agreement with certain key lenders.

- Tesla's global car sales fell sharply in the second quarter, continuing a decline that began last year, as the company puts a greater emphasis on autonomous driving instead of new models aimed at attracting car buyers.

- The Food and Drug Administration’s top vaccine official rejected broad uses of two Covid vaccines, citing unknown risks or injuries despite assurances of safety from dozens of staff experts, newly released documents show.

data.title: Parin Enterprises Gets Order Worth 77.9 Mln Rupees
data.description:
Parin Enterprises Ltd :

  • GOT ORDER WORTH 77.9 MILLION RUPEES

data.title: Domino's Pizza Enterprises Aiming to Accelerate Cost Cuts
data.description:
By Stuart Condie

SYDNEY--Domino's Pizza Enterprises' chief executive quit the troubled fast-food franchiser amid board concerns that his cost-reduction program wasn't moving quickly enough, the company's executive chair said.

The ASX-listed company, which runs the pizza brand in Australia, Japan and parts of Europe, this week said that Mark van Dyck would leave the business in December. He only took charge last November.

On Thursday, Executive Chair Jack Cowin said the board had broadly accepted van Dyck's five-year turnaround plan, which included shutting hundreds of underperforming restaurants. He said that some directors wanted faster results.

"We think that the costs can come down at a more reasonable rate without impacting on the sales growth number, but that has yet to be achieved," Cowin said on a call with analysts.

"If we're not going to get the sales growth, we're obviously going to play it different."

Cowin said that van Dyck hadn't been asked to resign and he had decided to quit. He said he believed that van Dyck might have found cutting costs and traveling to multiple countries too demanding.

Cowin highlighted technology as a leading target for cost reductions as the company aims to boost franchisee profitability. The company's proprietary technology is expensive and tech could be outsourced, he said.

Domino's Pizza Enterprises has no need to raise additional capital, Cowin added.

"Where we sit today is a good program for us to grow from," Cowin said.

Write to Stuart Condie at stuart.condie@wsj.com

data.title: Amtel Unit Ventures Into Manufacturing, Assembly of Motor Vehicles; Shares Drop 3%
data.description:
Amtel unit Amtel Cellular is foraying into the manufacturing and assembly of motor vehicles for the local and export markets, according to a Wednesday Malaysian bourse filing.

Shares dropped about 3% in Thursday midday trade.

The company will fund the preliminary costs and expenses via internally generated funds and/or bank borrowings, it said.

data.title: UK's Chesnara to buy HSBC Life UK for $355 million
data.description:
Life and pensions consolidator Chesnara on Thursday said it had signed an agreement to buy HSBC Bank's UK life insurance business, HSBC Life (UK) Ltd, for 260 million pounds($354.85 million).

($1 = 0.7327 pounds)

data.title: Reinet Investments, Athora Enter Advanced Talks for Pension Insurance Corp. Sale
data.description:
Reinet Investments (, RNI.JO) confirmed media reports that it is in advanced discussions with Athora Holding regarding the potential sale of its indirect 49.5% stake in Pension Insurance Corp. Group.

The proposed transaction implies a valuation of 5.7 billion pounds sterling for 100% of Pension Insurance's fully diluted share capital.

The investment manager noted that there is no guarantee that a deal will be finalized.

data.title: Rupee, forward premiums hoisted by uptick in Fed rate cut wagers
data.description:
The Indian rupee rose on Thursday after weak U.S. jobs data bolstered Fed rate cut bets, while a U.S.-Vietnam trade deal lifted regional sentiment.

Dollar-rupee forward premiums were helped by increased odds of rate cuts by the U.S. central bank as well with the 1-year implied yield touching an over one-month peak.

The rupee rose to 85.55 as of 11:30 a.m. IST, up 0.2% on the day.

Asian currencies were mostly rangebound while regional equities traded in the green, with India's benchmark indices, the BSE Sensex and Nifty 50 up about 0.4% each.

The odds of a Fed rate cut in July rose to one-in-four after a private sector payrolls report released on Wednesday surprised with the first fall in over two years.

Those odds were at about one-in-five before the data was released, per CME's FedWatch tool.

President Donald Trump also announced on Wednesday that the U.S. has struck a trade deal with Vietnam, including a 20% tariff on exports to the U.S. This helped fuel hopes that more deals may be struck ahead of the July 9 deadline.

U.S. and Indian negotiators are pushing try to land a tariff-reducing deal ahead of the deadline but some disagreements remained unresolved, sources familiar with the talks said.

Traders expect the rupee to be range-bound in the near-term with resistance pegged around 85.40 and support near the 86 handle.

A trade deal "would be a sentimental positive," for the rupee but substantial gains are likely only if it spurs a pick up in portfolio inflows, a trader at a foreign bank said.

Later in the day, the focus will be on the U.S. non-farm payrolls report for May which is expected to influence expectations around the future trajectory of benchmark policy rates.

"Quite bearish positioning on the broad US dollar index also suggests that a near-term squeeze on dollar shorts may be on the cards, though the broader trend is still for Asian currencies to continue strengthening," MUFG said in a note.

data.title: U.S. Eases Some Chip Software Curbs on China
data.description:
By Sherry Qin

The U.S. has lifted some curbs on exports of chip-design software to China, according to two companies that say they are working to restore access to recently restricted products.

California-based chip-design software developers Synopsys and Cadence Design both said they had been told by the U.S. Department of Commerce's Bureau of Industry and Security that recent restrictions on China-bound exports had been lifted.

"Cadence can confirm that BIS has rescinded the export restrictions set forth in the letter issued by BIS on May 23," it said in an emailed statement. "We are in the process of restoring access to our software and technology to affected customers in compliance with U.S. export laws."

Synopsys issued a similar statement on its website on Wednesday, adding that it is continuing to assess the impact of export restrictions related to China on its business.

Shares of both companies took a hit in late May when reports about the curbs emerged. Since then, tensions between the U.S. and China have eased. The two sides reached a trade truce in mid-June after talks in Switzerland, including vows to ease nontariff measures.

In exchange for China easing rare-earth curbs, U.S. negotiators agreed to relax some recent restrictions on the sale to China of certain products, The Wall Street Journal reported at the time.

"It is a positive step in that both sides appear to be taking action to uphold the agreements made in Switzerland, which improves odds that this cautious tariff ceasefire could hold," ING economist Lynn Song said.

But it remains difficult for the two sides to reach a grand bargain, he added.

Bloomberg reported Thursday that Germany's Siemens had also received a notice from the Commerce Department saying it is no longer required to seek government licenses for business in China.

The Commerce Department and Siemens didn't immediately respond to requests for comment.

Write to Sherry Qin at sherry.qin@wsj.com

data.title: South Korea's Parliament Votes to Amend Law to Boost Shareholder Rights
Russia Composite PMI Slips to 2-1/2-Year Low
data.description: The S&P Global Russia Composite PMI slumped to 48.5 in June 2025 from 51.4 in the previous month, marking the lowest reading since December 2022.
